topic: A person is asked to give a speech at their parent's retirement party
raw_output:
[story 1]

The microphone felt like a lead weight in my hand, cold and unforgiving against my palm. I stared out at the sea of smiling faces, each one a testament to the life my parents had built, and a crushing silence settled in my chest. My throat tightened, a physical constriction that made swallowing feel like sandpaper. I rehearsed the opening line in my head a dozen times, but the words dissolved into a meaningless static. Every glance I caught from my mother, a look of proud warmth, felt like a spotlight illuminating my own profound inadequacy. I wanted to bolt. I wanted the floor to swallow me whole. When I finally raised my eyes, they landed on my father, beaming, and a tremor ran through my hand, causing the notes to slip from my grasp. I mumbled something incoherent, the sound thin and pathetic, and then I simply walked off the stage, the applause washing over me like a physical wave, leaving me stranded on the cold tile floor backstage.

[story 2]

"So, I suppose I should start by saying..." The words emerged as a dry rasp, catching in my throat before they could fully form. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush that had nothing to do with the stage lights and everything to do with the sheer weight of expectation pressing down on my shoulders. My gaze darted everywhere but at the audience—at the ornate ceiling, at the overly polished silverware, at the clock ticking relentlessly toward an arbitrary end. My hands, slick with nervous perspiration, clenched into tight fists at my sides. *Just get through this,* I pleaded internally, a frantic, silent command. Each breath I took felt shallow, insufficient. When I finally managed to speak, my voice cracked on the first sentence, sounding foreign and brittle. I kept my eyes fixed on a spot just above the heads of the guests, terrified that if I looked down, I would shatter.

[story 3]

The stage felt cavernous, an abyss separating me from the happy noise emanating from the room. I adjusted the microphone stand with a jerky, almost violent motion, the metal cool against my fingertips. My mouth was bone dry. I looked at my parents, radiant and relaxed, and a sharp, painful ache bloomed behind my sternum. I tried to channel any sentiment, any memory, but all that surfaced was a hollow echo. My vision tunneled slightly at the edges. I felt a sudden, overwhelming urge to simply retreat into the shadows, to become invisible. When I opened my mouth to begin, a strange, paralyzing stillness overtook me. I could feel the blood draining from my face, leaving a clammy residue. I managed a weak nod, my whole body vibrating with a tension I couldn't seem to release, before I mumbled a rushed thank you and practically fled the area, needing air that didn't feel suffocating.
